flashcards.back: Multiple Instruction, Multiple Data -- each processor independently executes its own instruction stream on its own data stream. It is the most common and flexible parallel architecture.

flashcards.front: What is the main difference between shared memory and distributed memory MIMD systems?
flashcards.back: Shared memory: all processors access a common global address space (UMA or NUMA). Distributed memory: each processor has private local memory, communication via message passing.

flashcards.front: Name two programming models used for MIMD systems.
flashcards.back: OpenMP for shared-memory MIMD (thread-based, implicit data sharing) and MPI for distributed-memory MIMD (process-based, explicit message passing).

flashcards.front: What is cache coherence and why is it important in MIMD?
flashcards.back: Cache coherence ensures consistency of data across multiple processor caches. In shared-memory MIMD, when one processor writes to a cached location, other caches holding stale copies must be updated or invalidated.

flashcards.front: What does the 'E' stand for in the MESI protocol?
flashcards.back: Exclusive -- the cache has the only copy of clean data (identical to main memory). No other cache holds this line. It can transition to Modified (on write) or Shared (if another cache reads it).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between UMA and NUMA in shared-memory MIMD?
flashcards.back: UMA (Uniform Memory Access): all processors have equal memory access time (e.g., SMP systems). NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access): access time depends on whether memory is local or remote to the processor.

flashcards.front: What is memory contention in shared-memory MIMD?
flashcards.back: Memory contention occurs when multiple processors frequently access the same memory location or bus simultaneously, creating a performance bottleneck due to serialized access.

flashcards.front: Why is MIMD considered the most flexible parallel architecture?
flashcards.back: Each processor can execute completely different programs on different data simultaneously. It supports both data parallelism (same operation on different data) and task parallelism (different operations concurrently).

flashcards.front: What are examples of shared-memory MIMD systems?
flashcards.back: Multi-core CPUs (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen), multi-socket servers (dual/quad-socket Xeon), and symmetric multiprocessing (SMP) workstations.

flashcards.front: What are examples of distributed-memory MIMD systems?
flashcards.back: Beowulf clusters, supercomputers (Fugaku, Summit, Frontier), cloud computing platforms (AWS EC2, Google Cloud), and any cluster of networked machines running MPI programs.
